lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. facilities that repeatedly pressure- or thermally treat lumber, railroad ties, utility poles, posts, pilings, crossarms, and similar wood for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive treating operations, untreated sawmilling, chemical manufacturing, in-field remedial pole treatment classified outside the industry, non-operating property entities, and facilities lacking transferable permits or operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat batch product and toll-treatment sales to lumber distributors, railroads, utilities, infrastructure contractors, agricultural suppliers, and builders, generally priced per unit or volume with wood and preservative inputs reflected in quotes or contracts.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for combined NAICS 321100 rather than wood preservation alone; its largest roles are physical production, material moving, driving, supervision, and maintenance. I translate that broader occupational mix into a wage-weighted AI-task opportunity for treaters, adding exposure in inspection, charge recipes, compliance documentation, scheduling, procurement, and administration. Inclusion of sawmills likely overstates sawing-related opportunity and could understate chemical-process and compliance work.
a.rationale: Core work involves loading, pressure vessels, material handling, maintenance, sampling, and hazardous-chemical controls that are not readily substituted by AI. AI can assist vision inspection, process parameter recommendations, predictive maintenance, traceability, safety monitoring, paperwork, and commercial workflows.
a.change_evidence: A 321114-specific occupational census and time study separating manual from automated loading, treatment control, testing, environmental reporting, logistics, sales, and administrative tasks would materially change the range.
caveats: The available occupational source combines sawmills and wood preservation.
No national evidence quantifies current control-system, sensor, or vision adoption by independent treating-plant size.

rho.rationale: Digital quality records, scheduling, document automation, anomaly detection, and maintenance aids are implementable within five years. Integration with legacy retorts, safety validation, restricted-use pesticide rules, process certification, sparse labeled defect data, and limited technical staff constrain implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation cohorts from treating-equipment vendors or AWPA-member plants showing commissioning time, audit acceptance, false-alarm rates, operator overrides, and realized hours saved would narrow this range.
caveats: Regulation makes workflow automation valuable but raises the cost of errors.
This is bounded judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.

e.rationale: Most industry firms repeatedly treat products for external markets, but captive plants, facilities with non-transferable permits, legacy contamination, weak normalized earnings, customer concentration, or owner-dependent operations may fail the lens. Environmental diligence is a more material eligibility filter than in ordinary light manufacturing.
e.change_evidence: A facility-level review of the 57 frozen candidates covering ownership, external revenue, permit transferability, environmental reserves, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The injected n is margin-bridged from industry-average economics and may misclassify plants with volatile wood and chemical costs.
Eligibility can be lost through site-specific environmental liabilities that industry-level data cannot reveal.

s5.bridge: An SBA lumber-business transition case cites regional cross-industry surveys in which 57% of owners wanted to retire within five years and fewer than one in five had a credible succession plan. I apply a large haircut because neither the geography nor population is 321114, and retirement can lead to family transfer, employee ownership, closure, or continued work rather than a qualifying control sale. The proxy likely overstates qualifying transaction probability.
s5.rationale: A small, specialized facility population and rural ownership make succession pressure plausible, while remediation exposure, permits, capital intensity, and strategic acquisition by incumbents complicate transfers. The employee-ownership example also shows that a transition need not qualify as an external control sale.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook or GF Data transactions tagged to 321114, plus owner-age and succession-plan data from AWPA or the Railway Tie Association, would replace the broad retirement-intent proxy.
caveats: The source is a New York lumber case, not a national wood-preservation cohort.
No observed denominator of eligible plants or qualifying transfers is available.

q.rationale: Standard residential treated lumber faces price competition and periodic rebidding, so visible cost gains are shared. Compliance capability, treatment consistency, recovery, reduced chemical usage, reliable delivery, and qualification for utility or rail products can be less transparent and support greater retention, especially where approved suppliers are limited.
q.change_evidence: Five years of customer contracts, quote history, bid tabs, chemical and wood surcharge clauses, and gross margins before and after verified automation projects across residential, utility, and rail product lines would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Retention varies sharply between commodity decking lumber and specification-heavy rail, pole, piling, or marine products.
No public evidence directly measures automation-benefit pass-through in LMM treaters.

d5.rationale: Demand is diversified across residential outdoor lumber, remodeling, rail ties, poles, posts, and marine or civil structures. NAHB expects modest near-term single-family growth and real remodeling growth, while long replacement cycles for ties and poles stabilize industrial demand. Chemical restrictions, material substitution, housing weakness, and infrastructure timing create downside.
d5.change_evidence: AWPA or industry-vendor shipment forecasts split by residential lumber, ties, poles, posts, pilings, and preservative system, combined with utility and railroad replacement plans, would materially improve the five-year ratio.
caveats: Housing forecasts cover only part of the service basket and only the first two forecast years.
Rail and utility demand is inferred from durable end uses rather than an observed five-year volume forecast.

o.rationale: Pressure and thermal impregnation require controlled industrial equipment, certified applicators for restricted-use preservatives, worker protection, environmental controls, testing, and accountable product delivery. AI can improve plant work but cannot replace the responsible treatment operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers insourcing approved treating capacity at scale, autonomous certified plants, or rapid replacement of treated wood by steel, concrete, composites, or naturally durable species would lower the estimate.
caveats: Material substitution can eliminate some treated-wood quantity even though remaining treatment still requires an operator.
Permitting and certification are jurisdiction- and chemistry-specific.

narrative.executive_view: Wood preservation is a small, regulated process-manufacturing industry with durable repeat demand from construction and infrastructure. AI opportunity exists in quality, process advice, maintenance, compliance, logistics, and administration, but compensation is a small share of receipts and the physical hazardous-chemical process remains asset- and operator-dependent.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can detect treatment anomalies, recommend recipes, anticipate equipment failure, inspect material, sequence charges, and automate traceability and reports. Human accountability remains essential for loading, sampling, maintenance, certified pesticide application, safety, and environmental response.
narrative.value_capture: Approved-supplier status and quality consistency can protect some gains in utility, rail, and marine products. Commodity residential lumber is more exposed to competitive repricing, and input-linked contracts may reveal cost reductions.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen population is only 57 estimated firms, and environmental liabilities, permit transferability, captive ownership, and normalized earnings can shrink it substantially. Succession pressure is plausible but not directly measured for treaters.
narrative.demand_durability: Preservation extends the usefulness of wood in exposed settings, with repeat demand across remodeling, construction, rail, utility, agricultural, and marine uses. Alternative materials and chemical restrictions are the principal threats, while an accountable treatment operator remains necessary for nearly all surviving volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Critical risks are contaminated sites, worker exposure, preservative registration changes, product liability, customer concentration, housing cyclicality, wood and chemical input volatility, and a lack of direct adoption and transaction evidence for the small-firm population.
narrative.principal_driver: Regulated, specification-heavy treatment for long-lived infrastructure supports repeat, operator-required demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: A small firm universe and environmental-transfer risk constrain feasible acquisitions, while low labor intensity limits AI savings.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Shows the broader Sawmills and Wood Preservation occupation mix led by physical production, material-moving, driving, supervision, and maintenance roles.

sources.title: Wood Preserving Area Sources: National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants
sources.supports: Defines pressure and thermal wood preservation and details regulated pollutants, enclosed-vessel equipment, and management-practice requirements.

sources.title: Overview of Wood Preservative Chemicals
sources.supports: Documents restricted-use heavy-duty preservatives, commercial rail and pole uses, residential preservative systems, and ongoing registration review.

sources.title: Creosote
sources.supports: States that creosote protects railroad ties and utility poles, is applied using high-pressure equipment by certified applicators, and carries worker and ecological risks.

sources.title: 2026 Housing Outlook: Ongoing Challenges, Cautious Optimism and Incremental Gains
sources.supports: Forecasts single-family starts up 1% in 2026 and 5% in 2027 and real remodeling activity up 3% and 2%, respectively.

sources.title: Ward Lumber Transitions Ownership to Employees
sources.supports: A lumber-sector succession case citing regional surveys where 57% of owners sought retirement within five years and fewer than one in five had a credible succession plan.
